sections.heading: As the IFSC continues to evolve as a global ϐinancial hub, the sophistication of cyber
sections.title: Cyber security as foundational for financial stability in IFSC
sections.summary: This section establishes that cyber security is fundamental for financial entities operating in the GIFT IFSC, emphasizing its role in maintaining trust and stability. It mandates that regulated entities (REs) must implement cyber security measures to protect against threats like fraud, data breaches, and infrastructure disruptions. The guidelines require REs to align their security practices with the principle of proportionality based on operational scale, activity nature, ecosystem interconnectedness, and cyber risk exposure.
sections.dtia_summary: Cyber security is foundational for financial stability in IFSC; REs must implement proportionate security measures to protect against cross-border threats and ensure trust in financial services.

requirements.text: Cyber security is fundamental for financial entities in GIFT IFSC
Regulated entities must implement cyber security measures to protect against fraud, data breaches, and infrastructure disruptions
Cyber security practices must align with proportionality based on operational scale, activity nature, ecosystem interconnectedness, and cyber risk exposure

sections.source_quote: As the IFSC continues to evolve as a global financial hub, the sophistication of cyber threats targeting financial entities is also expected to grow. In such an international jurisdiction, where institutions cater to a diverse global client base, maintaining robust cyber security becomes fundamental.

sections.heading: The key components of the Guidelines are categorized into:
sections.title: Guidelines structured into five key components
sections.summary: This section categorizes the guidelines into five key components: Governance, Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework, Third Party Risk Management, Communication & Awareness, and Audit. It does not impose specific obligations but outlines the structural framework for the guidelines.

requirements.text: Guidelines are categorized into five key components: Governance, Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework, Third Party Risk Management, Communication & Awareness, and Audit

sections.source_quote: The key components of the Guidelines are categorized into: I. Governance II. Cyber security and cyber resilience framework III. Third party risk management IV. Communication & awareness V. Audit

sections.heading: The REs shall have adequate governance mechanisms, with a clear set of roles and
sections.title: Oversight Body for cyber risk management
sections.summary: This section mandates that regulated entities (REs) must establish an Oversight Body comprising stakeholders such as the Governing Board, senior management (including MD, CEO, CISO, CTO, etc.), or committees designated for cyber risk management. The Oversight Body is responsible for collectively managing cyber risk within the entity.
sections.dtia_summary: REs must establish an Oversight Body for cyber risk management, which includes governance structures to ensure cross-border data security and compliance.

requirements.text: REs shall have adequate governance mechanisms with clear roles and responsibilities for cyber risk management
Oversight Body includes Governing Board, senior management (MD, CEO, CISO, CTO, etc.), or committees designated for cyber risk management

sections.source_quote: The REs shall have adequate governance mechanisms, with a clear set of roles and responsibilities to manage cyber risk. The set of stakeholders involved in the governance of an RE's cyber risk management mechanism shall collectively be referred to as the 'Oversight Body' of the RE

sections.heading: The REs shall ensure that their Governing Board and the senior management possesses
sections.title: Senior management must cultivate cyber risk culture
sections.summary: This section requires that the Governing Board and senior management must possess sufficient expertise to understand and manage cyber risks, and they must set the tone for a strong culture of cyber risk awareness across all staff levels within the entity.
sections.dtia_summary: REs must ensure senior management cultivates a culture of cyber risk awareness to protect cross-border financial data.

requirements.text: Governing Board and senior management must possess sufficient expertise to understand and manage cyber risk
Governing Board and senior management must set the tone for a strong culture of cyber risk awareness across all staff levels

sections.source_quote: The REs shall ensure that their Governing Board and the senior management possesses sufficient expertise and knowledge to effectively understand and manage cyber risk.

sections.heading: The REs shall appoint a CISO or alternatively, designate a senior employee/
sections.title: Designated Officer for cyber risk management
sections.summary: This section mandates that REs must appoint a Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or designate a senior employee to assess, identify, reduce cyber security risks, respond to incidents, establish standards and controls, and direct the implementation of processes approved by the Oversight Body.
sections.dtia_summary: REs must appoint a Designated Officer to manage cyber risks, ensuring cross-border data security and compliance with IFSCA requirements.

requirements.text: REs shall appoint a CISO or designate a senior employee to assess, identify and reduce cyber security risks
Designated Officer must respond to incidents, establish appropriate standards and controls
Designated Officer must direct the establishment and implementation of processes approved by the Oversight Body

sections.source_quote: The REs shall appoint a CISO or alternatively, designate a senior employee/ management personnel to assess, identify and reduce cyber security risks, respond to incidents, establish appropriate standards and controls, and direct the establishment and implementation of processes and procedures as approved by the Oversight Body of the RE

sections.heading: The CISO or senior employee/ management personnel so designated shall henceforth
sections.title: Designated Officer designation
sections.summary: This section defines the 'Designated Officer' as the CISO or senior employee designated under Section 5, who is responsible for implementing cyber security measures as approved by the Oversight Body.

requirements.text: Designated Officer refers to the CISO or senior employee designated under Section 5

sections.source_quote: The CISO or senior employee/ management personnel so designated shall henceforth be referred to as the 'Designated Officer'.

sections.heading: The REs shall formulate the Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework to
sections.title: Cyber Security Framework for IT Asset Protection
sections.summary: Regulated entities must create a Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of their IT assets. This framework must include capabilities to anticipate and recover from cyber-attacks, assess cyber threats including third-party risks, define cyber risk appetite, outline people/process/technology requirements for risk management, establish roles during incidents, and be periodically reviewed for relevance. The framework applies to all regulated entities in the IFSC.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a comprehensive cyber resilience framework that directly impacts data transfer security by requiring entities to maintain robust incident response, third-party risk management, and continuous framework updates for cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: The REs shall formulate the Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework to maintain the Conϐidentiality, Integrity and Availability of their IT assets
The framework shall aim to maintain and promote the RE's ability to anticipate, withstand, contain, and recover from cyber-attacks
The framework shall take an integrated and comprehensive view of potential cyber threats, including third party risks
The framework shall define the RE's cyber risk appetite and cyber resilience objectives
The framework shall outline the RE's people, process and technology requirements for managing cyber risks
The framework shall establish roles and responsibilities of the Oversight Body, Designated Officer, employees and stakeholders during cyber incidents
The framework shall be reviewed and updated periodically to ensure relevance

sections.source_quote: 7. The REs shall formulate the Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience Framework to maintain the Conϐidentiality, Integrity and Availability of their IT assets.

sections.heading: The REs shall formulate an Information Security (IS) Policy as part of their cyber
sections.title: Information Security Policy with Risk-Based Controls
sections.summary: Regulated entities must develop an Information Security Policy as part of their cyber resilience framework with specific principles: maintain detailed IT asset inventories, conduct risk assessments and classify assets by business criticality, implement security controls aligned with international standards like NIST and ISO 27000, enforce least privilege access controls, ensure physical security, conduct annual vulnerability assessments, establish recovery policies, and define incident management procedures. The policy applies to all regulated entities in the IFSC.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires detailed security policies that directly affect data transfer by mandating asset classification, risk assessments, and third-party risk management for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: The REs shall maintain a detailed inventory of IT assets, including logical and physical components
The REs shall carry out a risk assessment of assets and classify them based on business criticality, data sensitivity, and potential impact
The REs shall identify and classify business functions and processes based on criticality to operations
The REs shall implement security controls aligned with international best practices and standards like NIST, ISO 27000
The REs shall ensure security controls are proportional to threat landscape and risk appetite
The REs shall manage access rights on a 'need-to-know' basis following least privilege and segregation of duties principles
The REs shall enforce robust authentication methods to ensure access control compliance
The REs shall ensure adequate physical security for IT assets
The REs shall conduct Vulnerability Assessment and Penetration Testing (VAPT) at least once a year
The REs shall have recovery policies and procedures to maximize service continuity and limit losses during disruptions
The REs shall define 'cyber incident' and develop processes for preventing, detecting, analyzing and responding to incidents
The REs shall establish mechanisms for fulfilling disclosure and reporting requirements during cyber incidents
The REs shall ensure audit trails exist for IT assets to satisfy business continuity, regulatory obligations, forensic evidence, and dispute resolution

sections.source_quote: 9. The REs shall formulate an Information Security (IS) Policy as part of their cyber security and cyber resilience framework with the following basic principles.

sections.heading: The REs shall adopt a collaborative security approach with their third-party
sections.title: Collaborative Third-Party Security Approach
sections.summary: Regulated entities must establish collaborative security practices with third-party vendors by clearly defining shared expectations for data security, incident reporting, and adherence to relevant security standards. This applies to all third-party relationships within the IFSC.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires explicit communication protocols for third-party data transfers, directly impacting cross-border data flows by mandating security standards and incident reporting mechanisms.

requirements.text: The REs shall adopt a collaborative security approach with third-party vendors/external partners
The REs shall clearly outline shared expectations for data security, incident reporting and adherence to relevant security standards

sections.source_quote: 10. The REs shall adopt a collaborative security approach with their third-party vendors/external partners, by clearly outlining shared expectations for data security, incident reporting and adherence to relevant security standards.

sections.heading: The REs shall adopt a risk-based approach for periodic review of third-party
sections.title: Risk-Based Third-Party Vendor Reviews
sections.summary: Regulated entities must implement a risk-based approach for periodic review of third-party vendors, identifying critical dependencies and conducting vulnerability assessments every six months for core operations. Less critical vendors may have flexible review frequencies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section directly impacts data transfer by requiring regular third-party risk assessments for critical systems, which is essential for cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: The REs shall identify third-party service providers critical to core operations or with access to critical systems
The REs shall carry out vulnerability assessments or compliance gap detection every six months for critical third parties
The REs shall determine appropriate review frequency for non-critical third parties

sections.source_quote: 11. The REs shall adopt a risk-based approach for periodic review of third-party vendors/external partners.

sections.heading: The REs should establish clear communication channels and escalation procedures
sections.title: Third-Party Risk Communication Channels
sections.summary: Regulated entities must establish clear communication channels and escalation procedures for addressing risks or non-compliance with third parties promptly and effectively.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures timely resolution of third-party data transfer issues, which is critical for maintaining data integrity in cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: The REs shall establish clear communication channels and escalation procedures for addressing identified risks or non-compliance with partners

sections.source_quote: 12. The REs should establish clear communication channels and escalation procedures for addressing any identified risks or non-compliance with partners, promptly and effectively.

sections.heading: The ultimate responsibility to mitigate the cyber risks arising from the third parties
sections.title: Third-Party Risk Responsibility
sections.summary: Regulated entities bear ultimate responsibility for mitigating cyber risks arising from third parties, ensuring comprehensive risk management across all vendor relationships.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that regulated entities are solely responsible for third-party cyber risks, which directly affects data transfer security and compliance for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: The ultimate responsibility to mitigate cyber risks arising from third parties shall be on the REs

sections.source_quote: 13. The ultimate responsibility to mitigate the cyber risks arising from the third parties shall be on the REs.

sections.heading: The REs shall provide regular training to its employees on topics pertaining to cyber
sections.title: Employee cyber security training requirements
sections.summary: Regulated entities must conduct regular cybersecurity training for employees covering phishing awareness, social engineering, password hygiene, and incident reporting procedures. This training is mandatory to ensure staff can identify and respond to cyber threats effectively. The training must be ongoing and address specific cyber risks relevant to the entity's operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regular employee training on cybersecurity to mitigate risks during data transfers and processing activities. This impacts DTIA by emphasizing staff awareness as a critical control for secure data handling.

requirements.text: Provide regular training to employees on cyber security topics
Include phishing awareness in training
Include social engineering awareness in training
Include password hygiene in training
Include incident reporting procedures in training

sections.source_quote: 14. The REs shall provide regular training to its employees on topics pertaining to cyber security, including but not limited to, phishing awareness, social engineering, password hygiene and incident reporting procedures.

sections.heading: The REs shall establish clear and accessible channels for employees to report
sections.title: Employee incident reporting channels
sections.summary: Regulated entities must establish clear and accessible methods for employees to report suspicious activities, vulnerabilities, and potential cyber incidents. This ensures timely identification and response to security threats. Employees can use these channels to escalate issues before they escalate into major breaches.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires mechanisms for employees to report cyber incidents, which is critical for DTIA as it ensures early detection and mitigation of data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Establish clear and accessible channels for employees to report suspicious activity
Establish clear and accessible channels for employees to report vulnerabilities
Establish clear and accessible channels for employees to report potential cyber incidents

sections.source_quote: 15. The REs shall establish clear and accessible channels for employees to report suspicious activity, vulnerabilities, and potential cyber incidents.

sections.heading: The aim of the audit is to provide an independent assurance of the effectiveness of
sections.title: Cybersecurity audit requirements
sections.summary: Regulated entities must undergo periodic cybersecurity audits by a certified auditor to ensure their security measures are effective. The audit must be conducted annually or more frequently based on cyber risk levels. The auditor must certify that security controls align with the entity's risk profile and submit findings to the IFSCA Governing Board and senior management.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates periodic cybersecurity audits to validate security controls, which is essential for DTIA as it ensures data transfer mechanisms are robust and compliant with security standards.

requirements.text: Conduct periodic audits of RE's cyber risk management measures
Audit by a CERT-In empanelled auditor
Audit by an independent auditor with CISA certification
Audit by an independent auditor with CISM certification
Audit by an independent auditor with GSNA certification
Audit by an independent auditor with CISSP certification
Audit by an auditor with prior experience in cybersecurity for similar entities
Certify that security controls align with RE's cyber risks

sections.source_quote: 16. The aim of the audit is to provide an independent assurance of the effectiveness of RE's measures to its Governing Board and the senior management. Towards this effect, the governance, systems and processes established by the REs for managing their cyber risks shall be audited on a periodic basis by either of the following: a. a CERT-In empanelled auditor, b. an independent auditor possessing the following certiϐications: i. Certiϐied Information Security Auditor (CISA) or ii. Certiϐied Information Security Manager (CISM) or iii. GIAC Systems and Network Auditor (GSNA) or iv. Certiϐied Information Systems Security Professional (CISSP)

sections.heading: The auditor engaged by an RE shall not have any conϐlict of interest with the RE. The
sections.title: Auditor conflict of interest and reporting
sections.summary: The auditor engaged by a regulated entity must not have a conflict of interest with the entity. The audit report must be submitted to IFSCA within 90 days of the financial year-end. Entities may conduct audits more frequently than annually based on their cyber risk levels and system criticality.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires auditor independence and timely reporting of audit results, which is vital for DTIA to ensure ongoing compliance with security standards during data transfers.

requirements.text: Auditor must not have any conflict of interest with RE
Submit audit report to IFSCA within 90 days of financial year-end
Submit audit report to IFSCA's corresponding department/division
Conduct audits at least annually
Conduct audits more frequently than annually based on cyber risk levels

sections.source_quote: 17. The auditor engaged by an RE shall not have any conϐlict of interest with the RE. The audit shall be conducted annually and a report in this regard shall be submitted to IFSCA by the REs within 90 days from the end of the ϕinancial year.

sections.heading: In case the RE is registered with IFSCA as a Broker Dealer/Bullion Trading Member,
sections.title: Audit report submission for market participants
sections.summary: Regulated entities registered as Broker Dealers, Bullion Trading Members, Clearing Members, or Depository Participants must submit their cybersecurity audit reports to IFSCA within 7 days of submitting to a Market Infrastructure Institution or Bullion Exchange. This ensures timely compliance with IFSCA's cybersecurity standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for streamlined audit reporting for market participants, which is relevant for DTIA as it ensures consistent security practices across financial entities during data transfers.

requirements.text: Submit cybersecurity audit report to IFSCA within 7 days of submission to Market Infrastructure Institution/Bullion Exchange

sections.source_quote: 18. In case the RE is registered with IFSCA as a Broker Dealer/Bullion Trading Member, Clearing Member/Bullion Clearing Member or as a Depository Participant, and is required to submit cyber security audit report to a recognised Market Infrastructure Institution/Bullion Exchange, such REs may submit the same audit report to IFSCA within 7 days of submission of the audit report to the Market Infrastructure Institution/Bullion Exchange.

sections.heading: In case of occurrence of any cyber incident, the REs are required to report the
sections.title: Cyber incident reporting timeline
sections.summary: Regulated entities must report cyber incidents to IFSCA's cyber incidents email address within six hours of detection. This includes details of the incident, its impact, and any mitigating actions taken. The reporting requirement ensures rapid response to incidents that could affect data security.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates immediate reporting of cyber incidents, which is critical for DTIA to address data transfer risks and ensure timely mitigation of breaches.

requirements.text: Report cyber incidents to cyber-incidents@ifsca.gov.in within six hours of detection
Copy report to IFSCA's CISO

sections.source_quote: 19. In case of occurrence of any cyber incident, the REs are required to report the particulars of the incident to the Authority on cyber-incidents@ifsca.gov.in with a copy to CISO, IFSCA, not later than six (6) hours from the detection of the incident.

sections.heading: Additionally, the REs shall submit the interim report within 3 days followed by a
sections.title: Interim reports and root cause analysis within deadlines
sections.summary: Regulated entities (REs) must submit an interim report within 3 days of an incident, followed by a detailed root cause analysis report within 30 days. REs must implement mitigation measures for the incident within 7 days. These obligations apply to REs operating in International Financial Services Centres (IFSCs) as per the guidelines.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely reporting and mitigation actions for incidents, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific timelines for root cause analysis and corrective measures across borders.

requirements.text: REs shall submit interim report within 3 days
REs shall submit detailed root cause analysis report within 30 days
REs shall take mitigation measures within 7 days

sections.source_quote: 20. Additionally, the REs shall submit the interim report within 3 days followed by a detailed root cause analysis report within 30 days. The REs shall take mitigation measures for the same within 7 days.

sections.heading: The following categories of REs are exempted from the requirements mentioned in
sections.title: Exempted categories of regulated entities
sections.summary: The following categories of regulated entities (REs) are exempt from the guidelines: REs operating as branches of regulated Indian or foreign entities; REs providing services to group entities only (e.g., Global In-House Centres); REs with fewer than 10 employees; and foreign universities established in IFSCs. These exemptions apply to REs operating in International Financial Services Centres.
sections.dtia_summary: Identifies specific REs exempt from compliance, which affects DTIA by reducing the scope of reporting and security requirements for certain entities operating in IFSCs.

requirements.text: REs operating as branches of regulated Indian or foreign entities
REs providing services to group entities only (e.g., Global In-House Centres)
REs with fewer than 10 employees
Foreign universities set up in IFSCs

sections.source_quote: 21. The following categories of REs are exempted from the requirements mentioned in these Guidelines: a. The REs operating in the form of a branch of a regulated Indian or foreign entity. b. The REs providing services to their group entities only e.g. Global In-House Centres (GICs). c. The REs which have less than 10 employees. d. Foreign universities set up in IFSCs.

sections.heading: The exemptions to the REs provided vide para 21, are subject to fulϐilment of the
sections.title: Conditions for exemptions from requirements
sections.summary: Exemptions granted under Section 21 are conditional on REs adopting the parent entity's Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience framework; designating the parent entity's CISO as the Designated Officer; ensuring the parent entity is regulated by a financial-sector regulator; and submitting certification within 90 days of each financial year. These exemptions are valid for three years from the guidelines' issuance date.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for exemption from compliance, which impacts DTIA by requiring specific alignment with parent entities' frameworks and timely certification for entities operating in IFSCs.

requirements.text: REs shall adopt Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience framework and IS Policy of parent entity
CISO of parent entity shall act as Designated Officer for REs in IFSC
Parent entity shall be regulated by financial-sector regulator in home jurisdiction
Parent entity's cyber security framework shall include REs in IFSC within scope
Designated Officer shall certify systems/processes in line with guidelines within 90 days of financial year end
Undertaking shall be submitted to IFSCA supervision division
Exemptions valid for three years from guidelines issuance

sections.source_quote: 22. The exemptions to the REs provided vide para 21, are subject to fulϐilment of the following conditions: a. REs shall adopt the Cyber Security and Cyber Resilience framework and IS Policy of its parent entity. b. The CISO of the parent entity shall act as the Designated Ofϐicer for the REs in IFSC. c. The parent entity of REs, in India or overseas, shall be regulated by a financial-sector regulator in its home jurisdiction and cyber security and cyber resilience framework of the parent entity shall include within its scope, the REs in IFSC. The Designated Ofϐicer of the RE shall certify that all the necessary systems/processes have been put in place, which are in line with these Guidelines within 90 days of the end of each financial year. The said undertaking shall be submitted to the respective Department/ Division of IFSCA responsible for supervision of the RE. These exemptions have been granted for a period of three years from the date of the issuance of these guidelines.
